statement: Finer grind size increases extraction yield by raising the coffee surface area exposed to water.
Finer grind size increases flow resistance, which raises brew pressure and slows the shot.
A sour shot is under-extracted; grinding finer or raising water temperature raises extraction yield to fix it.
A bitter shot is over-extracted; grinding coarser or lowering water temperature lowers extraction yield to fix it.
Extraction yield is measured as TDS times beverage mass divided by dry dose; target is roughly 18-22 percent.
Dose is the mass of dry coffee in the basket; higher dose in a fixed basket raises the puck density and flow resistance.
Brew ratio is beverage mass divided by dose; a 1:2 ratio is a typical modern espresso.
Higher water temperature increases extraction yield, especially of the slower-dissolving bitter compounds.
Water hardness (calcium and magnesium) increases extraction of flavor but scale forms above about 4 grains per gallon.
Scale buildup from hard water lowers the machine's effective heating and clogs the group, reducing brew pressure over time.
The pump targets about 9 bar of brew pressure at the puck for classic espresso.
Pre-infusion applies low pressure first to wet the puck evenly, reducing channeling before full pressure.
Channeling is water finding a low-resistance path through the puck, causing uneven and lower extraction yield.
Distributing and tamping level (puck prep) reduces channeling by making flow resistance uniform across the puck.
Roast level shifts solubility: darker roasts are more soluble and extract faster, so they often want a coarser grind.
Fresh beans off-gas CO2, which causes gushing and unstable flow for the first few days after roast.
Bean origin and process (washed vs natural) set the flavor ceiling; brewing can only reveal, not add, those notes.
Milk steaming introduces air first (stretching) then heats and folds it to build glossy microfoam.
Overheating milk past about 65 C scalds it, breaking down proteins and destroying microfoam and sweetness.
Higher-fat and higher-protein milk foams more stably, so whole milk holds microfoam longer than skim.
Shot time is an OUTPUT of grind, dose and pressure, not a target to dial directly; chasing a fixed time by feel hides the real levers.
Basket size sets the headspace above the puck; too much headspace lets the puck expand and channel.
Strength (TDS) is driven mainly by brew ratio, while flavor balance is driven mainly by extraction yield — two independent axes people conflate.
